@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 engine costs £4,500–£8,500 supply only, varying by generation: M119/M113 £3,000–£5,500, M273 £4,000–£7,000, M278 £5,000–£8,500, M176 £6,500–£11,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,000. A timing chain job costs £2,000–£4,500. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: That depends on which generation. The M119 and M113 are robust, well-developed V8s. The M273 (W221) has well-documented timing chain and balance shaft issues. The M278 (W222) is a powerful twin-turbo but has turbo cooling pipe and timing chain concerns. The M176 (W223) with EQ Boost is more robust but the 48V system adds complexity. Buy the newest you can afford.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: M273: timing chain and balance shaft issues (cold-start rattle, £2,000–£4,500). M278: turbo cooling pipe failure (coolant loss, £800–£2,000) and timing chain tensioner issues (£2,000–£4,500). M176: 48V EQ Boost system issues (£800–£2,500). All: engine mount wear (vibration, £600–£1,500).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: S-Class V8 values range from £5,000–£80,000+ depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £4,500–£8,500 reconditioned engine represents 10–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained W222 or W223, replacement is often justified. For an ageing W140/W220 with other issues, assess scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S-Class V8 has used five engine families: M119 (W140, 1991–1998, 4.2L), M113 (W140/W220, 1991–2005, 4.3L/5.0L), M273 (W221, 2005–2013, 4.7L/5.5L), M278 (W222, 2013–2020, 4.7L twin-turbo), and M176 (W223, 2020+, 4.0L twin-turbo with EQ Boost). Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M273 timing chain issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Timing chain and balance shaft replacement costs £2,000–£4,500 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£4,000–£7,000). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — ignored chain wear can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

eraMap.title: Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 279–302 bhp — reliable, chain-driven, SBC brake pump is a separate concern
382–388 bhp — timing chain and balance shaft are key risks
455–469 bhp — hot-vee V8, turbo cooling pipes are key watchpoint
496 bhp with EQ Boost — most advanced S-Class V8, 48V complexity

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across all S-Class V8 generations. Older M119/M113 engines are more affordable; the M176 with EQ Boost commands the highest premium. A typical S-Class engine replacement in the UK can range from £3,000 to £7,000 or more. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S-Class V8 engine typically takes 10–16 hours at £80–£120/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £140–£190/hr (London/SE dealer). The S-Class engine bay is spacious but the hot-vee design adds complexity on later models.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — coolant loss is serious
Restores smoothness — worth it on any S-Class

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8? Check These First
buyIf: * W140/W220 with M119/M113 — robust, proven V8s with good reliability
* Post-2012 M278 with documented timing chain and turbo cooling pipe history
* W223 S580 with full service history and 48V system health check
* Full service history with regular oil changes and Mercedes-approved oil
avoidIf: * M273 (W221) with any cold-start rattle — timing chain/balance shaft risk is serious
* M278 with any evidence of coolant loss — turbo cooling pipe failure risk
* M278 with cold-start rattle — timing chain tensioner risk
* Any S-Class V8 with poor service history

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The S-Class V8 is the flagship of the Mercedes range — the engine that defines luxury motoring. The M119 and M113 are robust, well-developed V8s. The M273 has well-documented timing chain and balance shaft issues. The M278 is a powerful twin-turbo but has turbo cooling pipe and timing chain concerns. The M176 with EQ Boost is the most advanced and more robust, but the 48V system adds complexity. Buy the newest you can afford, maintain the timing chain and cooling systems, and the S-Class V8 will reward you.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz S-Class V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain/balance shaft warning)
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — see the Era Map above
* Timing Chain History — M273/M278: chains and tensioners inspected or replaced?
* Coolant Level — M278: check for any loss — turbo cooling pipe failure risk
* 48V System (M176) — Any battery warnings? EQ Boost system health check
* Engine Vibration — Any shaking at idle? Engine mount wear is common
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and oil cooler
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
